critic_raw:
The privacy score S_pri is defined as a measure of how difficult reconstruction attacks find it to recover the original image from gradients computed over transformed data. This metric is attack-specific: a policy that defeats the particular reconstruction attack used during policy search may be entirely ineffective against a different attack family. The paper claims to find 'general policies capable of defeating various types of reconstruction attacks,' but if the search process only evaluated S_pri under one attack or a small fixed set of attacks, the generality claim is not supported — it may reflect policy overfitting to the specific attacker model used during search.

The S_acc metric (accuracy score) conflates two distinct properties: (1) whether the local model trains well on the transformed data, and (2) whether the global model produced by federated aggregation of gradients from transformed data converges to a good solution. Local accuracy is a necessary but not sufficient condition for global model quality under federated aggregation. Transformations that preserve local accuracy could still disrupt gradient alignment across heterogeneous clients, degrading federated convergence even when S_acc appears satisfactory.

The claim that policies 'transfer between different datasets' is a generalization claim that requires testing on datasets with substantially different visual statistics. If the source and target datasets share similar low-level statistics (e.g., both are natural image datasets), transferability may reflect incidental similarity rather than principled policy robustness. The mechanism by which a policy learned on one dataset defeats reconstruction attacks on another is not established.

The transformation policy is defined as a fixed augmentation pipeline applied before gradient computation. This is a static defense against an adaptive attacker. Any publication of the policy makes the defense trivially gameable: an attacker who knows the augmentation pipeline can invert or model it, effectively undoing the privacy protection. The evaluation implicitly assumes a non-adaptive attacker who does not update the attack model in response to observing the transformed gradients, which is unrealistic for real adversarial scenarios.
all_issues_raised: S_pri is evaluated against a fixed set of reconstruction attacks during policy search, so the 'general policies defeating various attacks' claim may reflect policy overfitting to the specific attacker models used in the search, not true generality.

S_acc measures local training accuracy on transformed data but does not verify global federated convergence — transformations could preserve local accuracy while disrupting cross-client gradient alignment.

The transferability claim between datasets is only supported if source and target datasets have substantially different visual statistics; transfer between natural image datasets with similar low-level structure does not demonstrate principled robustness.

The transformation policy is a static, published defense; any adaptive attacker who knows the augmentation pipeline can model or invert it, yet the evaluation assumes a non-adaptive attacker throughout.
all_issues_adjudicated: S_pri is evaluated against a fixed set of reconstruction attacks during policy search, so the 'general policies defeating various attacks' claim may reflect policy overfitting to the specific attacker models used in the search, not true generality.

S_acc measures local training accuracy on transformed data but does not verify global federated convergence — transformations could preserve local accuracy while disrupting cross-client gradient alignment.

The transferability claim between datasets is only supported if source and target datasets have substantially different visual statistics; transfer between natural image datasets with similar low-level structure does not demonstrate principled robustness.

The transformation policy is a static, published defense; any adaptive attacker who knows the augmentation pipeline can model or invert it, yet the evaluation assumes a non-adaptive attacker throughout.
